name: Hidden Classes and Inline Caches
summary: Self-derived design where dynamically-typed object access is accelerated via compile-time-discovered 'shape' classes plus per-call-site inline caches. V8, SpiderMonkey, JavaScriptCore all use it. 60–100× faster monomorphic property access than naive dictionary lookups; megamorphic cliff when polymorphism dominates.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Hidden classes** (V8 calls them "maps"; SpiderMonkey calls them "shapes") and **inline caches** are the canonical optimisation pair for dynamically-typed object access. The technique originated in Self (David Ungar, Craig Chambers) and propagated to every modern JavaScript and Lua engine.

Mechanism: each object's actual layout is tracked as a hidden class (or shape). Two objects with the same property names in the same order share the same hidden class. Inline caches at property-access sites record the (hidden class, offset) pair from a previous access; on subsequent calls, the cache checks if the new object's hidden class matches; if so, the access is a direct memory load at the cached offset.

sections.title: Monomorphic / polymorphic / megamorphic
sections.content:
**Monomorphic** call sites see only one hidden class — the cached fast path always hits, and access is essentially as fast as a static struct field load. 60–100× faster than naive dynamic dispatch.

**Polymorphic** call sites see a small number of hidden classes (typically <10). The IC chains a few cases; a small linear search hits one. Still much faster than dynamic dispatch.

**Megamorphic** call sites see many hidden classes. The IC fills, falls back to slow-path lookup. Performance drops by 10–100×. The "megamorphic cliff" is one of the JS performance sharp edges — code that touches objects with many distinct shapes loses the IC's benefit entirely.

For language designers: any dynamically-typed object system benefits from hidden classes plus IC. The key engineering challenge is the shape-transition graph that tracks how shapes evolve as properties are added; pre-V8 engines that didn't track this efficiently saw catastrophic shape-explosion on object construction patterns.
